candidates.direction_name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 PTX Grid
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest orthogonal family still open on top of the current best PTX base.
It is ranked ahead of another PTX control tweak because the latest exploit win did not materially change hot-band counters.
If this stays flat, the search can return to PTX-local tuning with little regret.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 3/20 recovered from the 256x128 loss and set a new best at 24.177664 ms, but the hot-band PTX kernel's NCU signature barely moved relative to the previous PTX base: kernel time remained about 32.687 ms and register count stayed at 200/thread. That suggests the unroll tweak was only a small perturbation rather than a structural feed breakthrough. The best next move is therefore a cheap orthogonal traversal/locality test on the proven PTX base. The current branch still launches a 60x50 hot-band grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can stay resident per SM, so a grouped-row or serpentine traversal remap is the best low-risk probe of inter-CTA locality.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and traversal efficiency on the current 128x128 PTX hot-band grid under low occupancy.
candidates.risk: Low to moderate. The surface is small and reversible, but previous CTA-order experiments have had mixed evidence, so the upside is bounded.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if one traversal tweak leaves hot-band time and cache-facing counters flat after a single measured round.

candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: Keep one live exploit family because it is still the current best performer.
This drops below traversal because the last measured win barely changed the hot-band signature.
If traversal is flat, this is still the next most coherent family.
candidates.hypothesis: The PTX exploit family is still healthy because it just set the best run, even if the measured counters barely moved. A second bounded exploit pass remains worth keeping in the set in case traversal changes also land flat. The next PTX-local tweak should stay tightly scoped to copy/order or export control in the PTX one-k hot-band kernel, not reopen geometry or shared-footprint changes.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX control-path overhead in the current one-k 128x128 hot-band branch.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best-known family, but repeated PTX micro-tweaks can easily devolve into noise if the counters stop moving.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if another PTX-local tweak also leaves the hot-band counters effectively unchanged.

candidates.direction_name: Reopen The Prior PTX One-K 128x128 Control Branch For A/B Guardrail
ranking_notes: Keep one explicit PTX-family guardrail because the current best and the prior PTX baseline are close enough that future noise matters.
This is below the forward-looking directions because it mainly preserves comparison quality.
Use it if the loop needs a clean PTX A/B reset.
candidates.hypothesis: The current best source is a PTX branch with the unroll-1 tweak layered on top. If the next rounds become noisy, the cleanest A/B control is to reopen the prior PTX one-k baseline that already measured 24.419329 ms. That keeps a known-good comparison point in the candidate set without reintroducing the failed 256x128 geometry family.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Not a new bottleneck attack; this is the PTX-family A/B restore guardrail.
candidates.risk: Low. This is a return to a previously measured PTX baseline variant.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 24.177664 ms base and the prior 24.419329 ms PTX run
candidates.stop_condition: Use this only if the higher-ranked directions become noisy or need a clean PTX-family control run.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
